text: The patient is positioned supine under general anesthesia. A transverse infraumbilical incision accesses the anterior rectus sheath, entering the preperitoneal space. Balloon dissection fills the space with carbon dioxide. Two 5mm trocars are placed in the suprapubic region. Lateral dissection of the space of Bogros reveals the iliopubic tract. An indirect inguinal hernia is identified on the left side, exposed with blunt and sharp dissection. The hernia sac is dissected from spermatic components and reduced into the abdominal space, with the peritoneum visibly pushed down. The medial and lateral areas are widened for mesh placement. Mesh is placed in the retroperitoneal area, covering potential hernia sites, and fixated with tags to the pubic tubercle and lateral abdominal wall.
The surgeon attempts extraperitoneal dissection of the right inguinal region but encounters widespread scar tissue and adhesions, making exploration unsuitable.

The surgeon accesses the peritoneal cavity and places trocars. An incision is made over the peritoneum in the right inguinal area, exposing anatomical landmarks. The indirect hernia sac is identified, separated from spermatic cord elements, and reduced into the abdominal cavity. Neurovascular structures and the vas deferens are observed to be intact. A biocompatible mesh is placed in the right inguinal region to cover potential hernia areas, and tags are used to fixate the mesh. The peritoneal defect is closed with barbed sutures.
The surgeon visually inspects the abdominal cavity, confirming no additional pathologic conditions. The trocars are removed under direct visualization, marking the completion of the operation.
